terms.definition: A React-based framework for building CLI apps with interactive components. Uses Yoga layout engine for flexbox-based rendering in the terminal. Version 4.x introduced hooks API and improved TypeScript support.

Text User Interface. A terminal-based interface that provides interactive elements (menus, forms, progress indicators) beyond simple command-line output. Contrasts with GUI (Graphical User Interface) and CLI (Command Line Interface with no interactive elements).
A Fabric knowledge repository at a specific location (e.g., ~/.fabric/stores/<id>/). Contains knowledge entries, metadata, and configuration. Isolated by UUID identity, independent of scope binding.

The relationship between a project workspace and a specific Store. A project MAY bind to multiple stores via required_stores configuration. Enables knowledge sharing across projects without co-location.

A discrete, atomic unit of work in the install/uninstall flow. Each stage MUST be idempotent (can be safely re-run), MUST have defined inputs/outputs, and SHOULD have visual representation in the TUI progress indicator.

The central ink-based component responsible for all user-facing output during install/uninstall operations. MUST support interactive (wizard) and non-interactive (CI) modes. Routes messages to appropriate visual components (spinners, text, boxes).

An interactive, step-by-step guided flow that prompts users for input and provides contextual help. Contrasts with command-line mode that accepts all parameters via flags. MUST support --non-interactive flag to disable wizard prompts.

A prominent visual element (symbol, color, or layout) that draws user attention to important state changes. Examples: ✓ checkmark for success, ✗ cross for error, ◐ spinner for in-progress. MUST be consistent across all pipeline stages.

An IETF specification that defines standard keywords for requirement documents: MUST (mandatory), MUST NOT (prohibited), SHOULD (recommended), SHOULD NOT (not recommended), MAY (optional). Used throughout Fabric documentation to clarify normative requirements.

The property that an operation can be applied multiple times without changing the result beyond the initial application. Pipeline stages MUST be idempotent: running `fabric install` twice MUST produce the same state as running once. Enables safe retry and resume behavior.

A condensed output block displayed at the end of install/uninstall operations. MUST be ≤15 lines in default mode, MUST include key metrics (files changed, hooks deployed, knowledge synced, duration), and SHOULD support --full-summary flag for detailed breakdown.

A mode where the system simulates operations without making actual changes. Used for previewing uninstall effects. MUST be activated via --dry-run flag and MUST display what would happen without executing.

Runtime detection of whether stdout/stderr is connected to an interactive terminal (TTY). When TTY is absent (piped to file or CI system), the OutputRenderer MUST adapt to non-interactive mode with simplified output and no ANSI color codes.

The ability to undo changes made by a pipeline stage upon failure. Implementations SHOULD support rollback for stages that modify files. Rollback MUST restore the system to the state before the stage began.
